description: Weld inspection methods, fit-up tolerances, NDE acceptance criteria, documentation, and common weld defects for pipe fitting QC

visual_inspection_VT.when: 100% of ALL welds — the first and most important inspection

root_pass: Full penetration (no lack of root penetration)
completed_weld: Weld profile — flush to slightly convex for butt welds, proper size for fillets
No undercut (max 1/32 inch for pressure piping)
No arc strikes on base metal
Weld properly cleaned — all slag removed
Weld width covers the groove fully
No excessive reinforcement (crown) — typically max 1/16 to 3/32 inch

tools: Weld gauges (hi-lo gauge, fillet gauge, reinforcement gauge, undercut gauge)
Borescope for internal inspection of small bore welds

liquid_penetrant_testing_PT.method: Apply penetrant to surface, allow dwell time, remove excess, apply developer, inspect for indications (bleed-back shows defects)
liquid_penetrant_testing_PT.detects: Surface-breaking defects only — cracks, porosity, lack of fusion at surface, laps
liquid_penetrant_testing_PT.does_NOT_detect: Subsurface defects (buried porosity, lack of fusion below surface, slag inclusions)
steps: 1. Clean surface — remove all paint, scale, slag, oil, grease. Wire brush and solvent clean.
2. Apply penetrant (red visible or fluorescent) — spray or brush evenly over test area
3. Dwell time — 10-30 minutes typical (longer for tight cracks in stainless). Minimum per procedure.
4. Remove excess penetrant — wipe with lint-free cloth dampened with cleaner. Do NOT spray cleaner directly on surface (washes penetrant out of defects).
5. Apply developer — thin even coat. White powder (dry developer) or spray.
6. Inspect after minimum development time (10-30 minutes) — look for red (or fluorescent) indications bleeding through developer
7. Evaluate indications — linear, rounded, or relevant per acceptance criteria
liquid_penetrant_testing_PT.temperature_limits: Penetrant materials rated 40F to 125F standard. Special materials for high/low temp.
liquid_penetrant_testing_PT.when_used: Socket welds, attachment welds, nozzle welds, branch connections, areas where RT is impractical, stainless steel welds, on top of root pass before fill (some specs), final surface of any weld per ITP

acceptance_per_ASME_B31_3.no_cracks: NO cracks — any crack is rejectable regardless of size
acceptance_per_ASME_B31_3.linear_indications: Max 1/16 inch for thickness under 3/4 inch, max 3/32 inch for thickness 3/4 inch and over
acceptance_per_ASME_B31_3.four_or_more: 4 or more indications in a line separated by less than 1/16 inch edge-to-edge = rejectable

magnetic_particle_testing_MT.method: Magnetize area, apply iron particles (dry powder or wet fluorescent) — particles cluster at defects that interrupt magnetic field
magnetic_particle_testing_MT.detects: Surface and NEAR-surface defects — cracks, lack of fusion, inclusions close to surface
magnetic_particle_testing_MT.limitation: Only works on ferromagnetic materials (carbon steel, chrome-moly). Does NOT work on stainless steel, aluminum, or non-ferrous metals. For stainless, use PT.

methods.yoke: Electromagnetic yoke — most common field method. Portable, no risk of arc strike.
methods.prods: Direct current through prods — RISK OF ARC STRIKES. Many project specs prohibit prods on pressure piping.
methods.coil: Wrap coil around pipe — good for circumferential welds

steps: 1. Clean surface — remove slag, scale, paint. Must be clean and dry.
2. Apply magnetizing force (yoke or other method)
3. Apply magnetic particles while magnetized — wet fluorescent (UV light needed) or dry visible (use in white light)
4. Inspect for indications — particles accumulate at defects
6. Demagnetize if required (critical for equipment that will be near instruments or for materials going into service where residual magnetism causes problems)
magnetic_particle_testing_MT.acceptance_per_ASME_B31_3: Same as PT criteria — no cracks, linear and rounded indication limits apply

radiographic_testing_RT.method: Radiation source (X-ray tube or gamma source like Ir-192, Co-60) on one side of weld, film or digital detector on other side. Radiation passes through weld — defects show as density changes on film.
radiographic_testing_RT.detects: Volumetric defects — porosity, slag inclusions, lack of fusion, incomplete penetration, cracks (if oriented properly)
radiographic_testing_RT.limitation: Planar defects (cracks, lack of fusion) oriented parallel to radiation beam may be missed. Cannot inspect fillet welds effectively. Cannot inspect socket welds.

safety.radiation: EXTREMELY DANGEROUS — radiation exposure can cause cancer and death. ONLY qualified radiographers handle sources.
safety.exclusion_zone: Rope off area with radiation warning signs and flashing lights. Typically 50-150 ft radius depending on source and setup.
safety.schedule: Often done on night shift or off-hours to minimize personnel exposure. Swing/night shift RT = coordinate with all trades.
safety.badges: All personnel in potential exposure area must wear dosimeters/film badges.
safety.if_you_see_source: If you see an unshielded gamma source (small metal cylinder, usually on cable or guide tube) — LEAVE THE AREA IMMEDIATELY and notify radiation safety.

film_quality.IQI_penetrameter: Image Quality Indicator placed on source side — must be visible on film to verify technique quality
film_quality.density: Film density 1.8-4.0 for adequate reading (2.0-3.5 preferred)

slag_inclusions.elongated: Max length: Lesser of 1/3T or 3/4 inch for T up to 3/4 inch. Lesser of 1/3T or 2 inches for T over 3/4 inch.
slag_inclusions.isolated: Max width 2/3T or 3/4 inch whichever is less

porosity.individual_pore: Max size: Lesser of 1/4T or 1/16 inch for T under 1/4 inch. 1/4T or 3/16 inch for T 1/4 to 3/4 inch.
porosity.cluster: Total area of porosity in any 6-inch length not to exceed 1% of area
porosity.distribution: Per Table in B31.3 — depends on wall thickness

incomplete_penetration.IP_without_concavity: Permitted up to limits based on thickness and aggregate length per Table
incomplete_penetration.IP_with_concavity: Rejectable if greater than specified limits

acceptance_per_ASME_B31_3_Table_341_3_2.cracks: NO cracks permitted — any crack visible on RT is rejectable
acceptance_per_ASME_B31_3_Table_341_3_2.lack_of_fusion: Generally rejectable unless very small and within specific limits per code

ultrasonic_testing_UT.method: Sound waves transmitted into weld — reflections from defects displayed on screen. Operator interprets signals to identify defect type, size, and location.
ultrasonic_testing_UT.detects: EXCELLENT for planar defects — cracks, lack of fusion. Also detects porosity, slag inclusions.
advantages_over_RT: Detects planar (crack-like) defects better than RT
No radiation hazard — can be done while other trades work nearby
Can determine depth and through-wall extent of defects
Works on thick sections where RT is impractical
limitations: Highly operator dependent — requires skilled technician
Difficult on thin wall (under 5/16 inch for shear wave)
Difficult on coarse-grained materials (some castings, austenitic stainless welds)
Documentation is less intuitive than RT film (no permanent picture of defect)
TOFD and phased array methods address some limitations but require more advanced equipment

advanced_methods.TOFD: Time of Flight Diffraction — uses diffracted signals for sizing. More accurate crack sizing than conventional UT.
advanced_methods.phased_array: Multiple element transducer — can sweep angles electronically. Faster scanning, better coverage, produces image (S-scan).
advanced_methods.AUT: Automated UT — mechanized scanning for consistent results. Common on pipeline girth welds as alternative to RT.

ultrasonic_testing_UT.acceptance: Per ASME B31.3 Table 344.6.2.1 or project specification — based on indication length and amplitude relative to reference reflectors

internal_misalignment_hi_lo.unequal_wall: Thinner wall must not be less than thicker wall minus 1/16 inch (may need to taper thicker pipe 30 degrees)
internal_misalignment_hi_lo.project_spec_often: 1/32 inch for critical service (chrome-moly, stainless high integrity, lethal service)

butt_welds_ASME_B31_3.root_gap: Per WPS — typically 1/16 to 3/32 inch for GTAW root, 3/32 to 1/8 inch for SMAW root, 0 for consumable insert
butt_welds_ASME_B31_3.out_of_round: Less than 1% of pipe OD at weld joint

socket_welds.gap: 1/16 inch between pipe end and socket bottom — MANDATORY. Prevents stress cracking from thermal cycling.
socket_welds.fillet_size: Minimum equal to wall thickness of pipe or fitting socket, whichever is thinner. Often specified as 1.09x Tn minimum.
socket_welds.passes: Minimum 2 passes per ASME B31.3 (B31.1 requires 2 passes as well)

branch_connections.set_on: Branch pipe sets ON header — no protrusion through header wall (unless reinforced set-through per drawing)
branch_connections.full_penetration: Most project specs require full penetration groove weld at branch-to-header connection
branch_connections.contour: Weld profile must have smooth transition to base metal — no sharp notches

flange_alignment.parallel: 1/16 inch per foot of flange OD maximum gap variation around circumference
flange_alignment.perpendicular_to_pipe: Flange face must be square to pipe centerline — check with straightedge on face and level on pipe

documentation_requirements.weld_map: Shows location and joint number of every weld in the system. Traceable to welder, WPS, NDE reports, and MTRs.
documentation_requirements.welder_qualification_record: WPQ per ASME IX on file for every welder. Must cover the process, position, material P-number, filler metal F-number, and thickness range being welded.
documentation_requirements.WPS: Welding Procedure Specification on file for every joint type. Must be qualified by PQR.
documentation_requirements.MTR_CMTR: Material Test Report / Certified MTR for every heat of material (pipe, fittings, flanges, bolts, gaskets where required). Traces material to mill/manufacturer.

NDE_reports.RT: Film/digital images stored, reader sheet documenting each weld examined, acceptance/rejection, technician certification
NDE_reports.UT: Scan data, calibration records, indication evaluation, technician certification
NDE_reports.VT: Inspection report with acceptance/rejection, inspector certification

contents: Witness signatures (contractor QC, owner inspector, third party if required)
Torque tool ID and calibration date
Item tested (weld, pipe, fitting, flange, bolt)

porosity.appearance: Gas pockets in weld — round voids visible on RT or cross-section
causes: Contamination — oil, grease, moisture, paint on base metal or filler
Wet or damaged electrodes (especially 7018 not kept in oven)
Wind blowing away shielding gas (GTAW, GMAW, FCAW-G)
Insufficient gas flow or gas contamination
Excessive arc length (SMAW)
Moisture in purge gas lines
Rusty filler wire
porosity.prevention: Clean thoroughly before welding. Keep electrodes dry. Protect from wind. Proper gas flow. Clean filler wire.

slag_inclusions.appearance: Non-metallic material trapped in weld — shows as dark irregular shapes on RT
causes: Inadequate cleaning between passes — not removing all slag before next pass
Too narrow a groove — slag trapped in tight corners
Welding over undercut without cleaning — slag caught in undercut
Improper weaving technique — slag rolls ahead of puddle
Welding too cold — slag doesn't float to surface
slag_inclusions.prevention: Thorough inter-pass cleaning (grinder + wire brush). Proper groove geometry. Adequate heat input.

lack_of_fusion_LOF.appearance: Weld metal not fused to base metal or previous pass — planar defect, very dangerous
causes: Insufficient heat input — too fast travel speed or too low amperage
Improper electrode angle — not directing arc into sidewall
Cold base metal — need preheat
Oxide on joint surface (especially stainless and aluminum)
Groove too narrow for electrode access
lack_of_fusion_LOF.prevention: Proper amperage and travel speed. Correct electrode angle. Adequate preheat. Clean surfaces. Proper groove design.

incomplete_penetration_IP.appearance: Root of weld not fully penetrated through joint — visible on internal surface or RT
causes: Root gap too tight — electrode can't reach root
Root face too thick — need thinner land or larger gap
Insufficient heat at root — low amperage or fast travel
Hi-lo misalignment blocking root access
Electrode too large for root gap
incomplete_penetration_IP.prevention: Proper root gap and root face per WPS. Correct amperage. Good fit-up (hi-lo within tolerance). Correct electrode size.

types.hot_cracks: Occur during solidification — centerline cracking from high sulfur/phosphorus, crater cracks from not filling crater
types.cold_cracks_hydrogen: Occur hours to days after welding — caused by hydrogen, high restraint, and susceptible microstructure. THE most dangerous defect.
types.PWHT_reheat_cracks: Occur during PWHT — more common in thick-section chrome-moly and some nickel alloys

causes_cold_cracking: Hydrogen from moisture in electrodes (7018 not kept in oven — #1 cause)
Cooling too fast after welding (high restraint, cold weather, wind)
Susceptible microstructure (high-carbon steel, chrome-moly without PWHT)
High residual stress (thick sections, rigid joints)
cracks.prevention: Keep electrodes dry (oven!). Preheat per WPS. Maintain interpass temperature. Slow cool (preheat blankets after welding). PWHT when required. Use low-hydrogen processes.

undercut.appearance: Groove melted into base metal along weld toe — reduces wall thickness
causes: Excessive amperage
Too long an arc
Incorrect electrode angle
Travel speed too fast
Excessive weaving
undercut.acceptance: Max 1/32 inch depth for pressure piping (some specs less). Sharp undercut worse than rounded.
undercut.prevention: Correct amperage. Proper technique. Slow down at toes of weld.

burn_through.appearance: Hole melted through root of weld — excessive penetration
causes: Too much heat (high amperage)
Too slow travel speed
Root gap too wide
Root face too thin
Thin-wall pipe
burn_through.prevention: Reduce amperage. Increase travel speed. Proper fit-up. Correct root face dimension.

arc_strikes.description: Inadvertent arc contact on base metal outside the weld zone — creates hard spot and potential crack initiation
arc_strikes.problem: Creates untempered martensite on alloy steels. Stress concentration and corrosion initiation site.
arc_strikes.code_requirement: Arc strikes on pressure piping must be ground smooth and examined by MT or PT. Some specs require grinding to remove all affected material.
arc_strikes.prevention: Use proper technique. Strike arc in the groove only. Use run-off tabs for practice starts.
